questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses BOTH the preterite and imperfect?
questions.explanation: Background state (sleeping) = imperfect: dormía. Interrupting event (phone rang) = preterite: sonó. This is the core preterite/imperfect pattern.

questions.prompt: What is the yo preterite of 'hacer'?
questions.explanation: 'Hacer' has the i-stem 'hic-'. Yo form: hice. Él form: hizo (c→z before o). This is one of the most important irregular preterites.

questions.prompt: Choose ser or estar: Mi abuela ___ de Veracruz y ahora ___ muy enferma.
questions.explanation: Origin = ser (es de Veracruz). Temporary health state = estar (está muy enferma). Classic ser/estar contrast.

questions.prompt: Fill in the double object pronoun: ¿Los boletos? ___ compré esta mañana. (I bought them for you)
questions.explanation: Indirect (to you = te) + direct (los boletos = los) = te los. Indirect always first. 'Se' only replaces 'le/les', not 'te'.

questions.prompt: Which reflexive verb sequence describes a morning routine correctly?
options: Me despierto, me levanto, me ducho y me visto.
Me despierto, me levanto, visto y ducho.
Despierto, levanto, me ducho y me visto.
Me despierto, levanto, me ducho y visto.
questions.explanation: All four verbs in a morning routine are reflexive: me despierto (despertarse), me levanto (levantarse), me ducho (ducharse), me visto (vestirse).

questions.prompt: How would you say 'I met Carlos for the first time at a party'?
options: Conocí a Carlos en una fiesta.
Conocía a Carlos en una fiesta.
Supe a Carlos en una fiesta.
Encontré a Carlos en una fiesta.
questions.explanation: 'Conocer' in the preterite = to meet for the first time. 'Conocía' (imperfect) would mean you already knew him. This is one of the key meaning-changing verbs.

questions.prompt: At a Mexican tianguis, a vendor says '¡Pásele, pásele!' What should you do?
options: Come over and look at their goods — it's an invitation to browse
questions.explanation: '¡Pásele!' is a friendly vendor call meaning 'Come on in!' It's an invitation to approach and browse, not a demand or pressure to buy.

questions.prompt: Which statement best describes an A2 Spanish speaker?
options: Can handle everyday situations, talk about the past, shop, travel, and understand common expressions in Mexican Spanish
Can discuss complex political and philosophical topics fluently
Can only say basic greetings and numbers
Can read Spanish literature without a dictionary
questions.explanation: A2 (Elementary) means you can communicate in routine tasks, describe past events, handle practical situations like shopping and travel, and understand common everyday expressions — exactly what this level has covered.
